title: Website Examples
description: Explore eight real-world website types you can build with React, ranging from simple portfolios to advanced chat applications. Each example includes a working code sample, the technologies involved, and notes on what a production version would add.

blocks.heading: Website Examples
blocks.body: One of the best ways to learn web development is to study real website types and build simplified versions of them. Below are eight common types of websites, each with a description, a working React code example, a list of technologies used, and notes on what a real production version would include.

A personal portfolio is a website that shows off who you are and what you have built. It is one of the most common first projects for new developers because it is simple, useful, and looks great on a resume. You typically include your name, a short bio, and cards for each project you have worked on.

blocks.description: A simple portfolio component with project cards

blocks.heading: What a real version would add
items: A contact form so visitors can reach you
Animated page transitions for a polished feel
Hosting on Vercel or Netlify so anyone can visit your site
Links to your GitHub, LinkedIn, and other social profiles

blocks.body: A blog lets you write articles and share them with the world. Blog projects teach you how to work with lists of data, how to create detail pages for individual posts, and how to organize content with categories. Many professional developers keep a blog to share what they learn.

blocks.description: A blog with post listing and detail view using useState

blocks.heading: What a real version would add
items: A database to store posts (like Supabase or a headless CMS)
Markdown support so you can write posts in a simple format
Categories and tags for filtering posts
A search bar to find posts by title or content
RSS feed so people can subscribe to your blog

blocks.body: An online store lets people browse products, add them to a shopping cart, and check out. This is one of the most common types of websites on the internet. Building one teaches you about managing state across multiple components (the cart needs to be available everywhere) and handling user interactions like adding and removing items.

blocks.description: An e-commerce store with product cards and a shopping cart

blocks.heading: What a real version would add
items: A real payment system like Stripe to process credit cards
User accounts so shoppers can track their orders
A database to store products, inventory, and order history
Shipping address form and delivery calculation

blocks.body: A dashboard is a page that shows important data at a glance using cards, numbers, and charts. Businesses use dashboards to monitor sales, website traffic, user activity, and more. Building a dashboard teaches you how to lay out data in a clear, organized way and how to work with chart libraries.

blocks.description: A dashboard with stat cards and a simple bar chart

blocks.heading: What a real version would add
items: Real-time data from APIs that updates automatically
Interactive charts you can hover and click on (using Recharts or Chart.js)
Date range pickers to view data for different time periods
Export to CSV or PDF functionality
User authentication so only authorized people see the data
Responsive layout that works on mobile devices

blocks.body: Social media apps let users create posts, like content, and leave comments. Think of a simplified Instagram or Twitter. Building one teaches you about user interactions, managing lists of data that change over time, and how features like "liking" a post work under the hood.

blocks.description: A social feed with posts, likes, and comments

blocks.heading: What a real version would add
items: User sign-up and login with profile pages
A database to store posts, likes, and comments permanently
Real-time notifications when someone likes or comments on your post
A follow system so you only see posts from people you follow
Infinite scrolling to load more posts as you scroll down

blocks.body: A SaaS (Software as a Service) landing page is a marketing website designed to convince visitors to sign up for your product. It typically has a hero section with a headline, a features section, a pricing table, and call-to-action buttons. Every startup needs one, making this a very practical skill to learn.

blocks.description: A SaaS landing page with hero, features, and pricing sections

blocks.heading: What a real version would add
items: Smooth scroll animations as sections come into view
A testimonials section with real customer quotes
Mobile-responsive layout that adjusts for phone screens
Analytics tracking to see which sections visitors spend time on
A newsletter signup form connected to a mailing list service

blocks.body: A chat application lets users send and receive messages in real time. Think of Discord, Slack, or iMessage. Building a chat app teaches you about real-time communication, managing a scrolling list of messages, and handling user input. It is a great way to learn about WebSockets and live data.

blocks.description: A chat application with message bubbles and real-time input

blocks.heading: What a real version would add
items: WebSockets for real-time message delivery without refreshing
Multiple chat rooms or direct messages

blocks.body: Web games and interactive apps are a fantastic way to learn React because they are pure fun. If you have ever played Roblox or Minecraft, you already understand the idea of building interactive experiences. Web games use the same React concepts (state, events, rendering) but apply them to things like scores, timers, and player actions.

blocks.description: A click speed challenge game with timer and high score tracking

blocks.heading: What a real version would add
items: Sound effects when you click or when time runs out
Animations and particle effects for visual flair
A leaderboard where players compete for the top score
Multiple game modes (timed, survival, target practice)
Mobile touch support so it works on phones
Save progress using localStorage or a database

keyPoints: Portfolio websites are ideal beginner projects that showcase your work and teach component composition
Blogs teach you about lists, detail pages, and content organization with categories
E-commerce stores introduce state management across components with shopping cart functionality
Dashboards teach data visualization with stat cards and chart libraries
Social media apps demonstrate user interactions like likes, comments, and dynamic lists
SaaS landing pages are practical marketing sites with hero sections, features, and pricing tables
Chat applications introduce real-time communication concepts with WebSockets
Games and interactive apps apply React concepts like state, events, and effects to fun experiences
